Title: Rhizobitoxine modulates plant–microbe interactions by ethylene inhibition

Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transcript analysis in inoculated transgenic plants with reduced ethylene sensitivity
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: increased upon inoculation in ethylene-insensitive transgenic plants
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: increased upon inoculation in ethylene-insensitive transgenic plants
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Mesorhizobium loti inoculation; altered ethylene sensitivity
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher NIN transcript levels are associated with increased formation of infection threads under reduced ethylene sensitivity.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NIN is described as a gene governing formation of infection threads. 2. Its transcript level increased in inoculated ethylene-insensitive Lotus plants. 3. This supports that ethylene suppresses early nodulation processes.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: NIN is included as a nodulation-related downstream marker rather than a primary metabolism-focused core gene in this review.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Inhibition of ACC synthase lowers ethylene evolution and promotes nodulation.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ACC synthase is strongly inhibited by rhizobitoxine. 2. It is the rate-limiting enzyme in plant ethylene biosynthesis. 3. Inhibition explains reduced ethylene evolution in host roots and enhanced nodulation.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Ethylene inhibits infection and nodulation in many legumes.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This is a host target enzyme rather than a focal biosynthetic gene of rhizobitoxine-producing bacteria.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Targetable via inhibitors such as AVG to improve transformation or nodulation contexts.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Rhizobitoxine irreversibly inhibits β-cystathionase in methionine biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. β-cystathionase was the first biochemical target identified for rhizobitoxine. 2. Rhizobitoxine acts as a cystathionine analog and irreversibly inhibits this enzyme. 3. The target links rhizobitoxine to methionine metabolism, though the review focuses mainly on ethylene-mediated symbiosis.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Methionine is a precursor for S-adenosylmethionine and downstream ethylene biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not a focal core gene of the review but a biochemical target relevant to nutrient metabolism.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: required for biosynthesis of target product
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Required for rhizobitoxine biosynthesis in culture and in planta; specific catalytic reaction not defined in this review
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Tn5-induced mutant analysis; deletion mutant; complementation analysis; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: symbiosis with legumes; in culture; in planta
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: rtxC; downstream ORFs in putative rtx operon
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. rtxA was identified from Tn5-induced rhizobitoxine-null mutants of B. elkanii USDA61. 2. rtxA is necessary for rhizobitoxine biosynthesis in culture and in planta. 3. Loss of the rtxA-containing locus abolishes production of rhizobitoxine and intermediates.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes rhizobitoxine production, which indirectly decreases host ethylene biosynthesis and enhances nodulation competitiveness.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Inhibits plant ACC synthase and thereby lowers ethylene, enhancing nodulation; also associated with chlorosis/phytotoxicity.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Rhizobitoxine is a phytotoxin causing chlorosis in soybean leaves.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequence comparison mentioned for Xanthomonas oryzae KACC10331; accession AE013598
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: In this review, the exact enzymatic function of RtxA is not resolved, only its requirement for biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for engineering rhizobia or other plant-associated bacteria to enhance nodulation by enabling rhizobitoxine production.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms target product from immediate precursor
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: deletion mutant; complementation analysis; metabolite profiling; in planta phenotype analysis
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: symbiosis with legumes; in culture; in planta
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: rtxA; downstream ORFs in putative rtx operon
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. rtxC is located immediately downstream of rtxA in the rtx operon. 2. rtxC is involved in desaturation of dihydrorhizobitoxine into rhizobitoxine. 3. Desaturation by the rtxC-encoded protein is essential for nodulation competitiveness and chlorosis phenotypes in planta.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes formation of active rhizobitoxine, thereby enabling ethylene inhibition, nodulation competitiveness, and chlorosis induction.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Inhibits plant ACC synthase and thereby lowers ethylene, enhancing nodulation; also causes chlorosis/phytotoxicity.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Active rhizobitoxine can induce chlorosis in soybean.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequence comparison with Xanthomonas oryzae KACC10331; accession AE013598
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Dihydrorhizobitoxine alone did not affect nodulation competitiveness or chlorosis induction, underscoring the importance of the rtxC-dependent desaturation step.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target for metabolic engineering when transferring rhizobitoxine biosynthesis capacity to improve symbiotic performance.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: competes with target-product branch by depleting precursor
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Degrades ACC, lowering the precursor pool available for plant ethylene biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: genome-based candidate identification; disruption mutant; nodulation and competitiveness assays
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ACC deaminase gene mlr5932 was identified from the M. loti MAFF303099 genome as a candidate ethylene-lowering factor. 2. Disruption of mlr5932 reduced nodulation enhancement and competitiveness. 3. ACC deaminase represents an alternative strategy to rhizobitoxine for reducing host ethylene.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Decreases host ethylene precursor ACC and enhances nodulation and competitiveness.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Ethylene negatively regulates rhizobial infection and nodulation in many legumes.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome database search of M. loti MAFF303099 (Kaneko et al., 2000)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review also notes ACC deaminase genes in Rhizobium leguminosarum and Bradyrhizobium japonicum.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Recommended for introduction into rhizobial strains lacking ethylene-decreasing activity to improve symbiotic interactions.

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: signal perception impairment; dominant reduction of ethylene sensitivity
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: ethylene signaling pathway; NIN transcript accumulation indirectly
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Reduced ethylene perception is associated with increased NIN transcripts after inoculation
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: condition-dependent controller of nodulation through ethylene perception
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic expression of mutant receptor; inoculation assay; infection thread and nodule primordia quantification; transcript analysis
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: ethylene receptor signaling complex not specifically detailed
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: transgene expressed in transformed L. japonicus roots/plants
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Ethylene inhibits infection thread progression and nodule primordium formation.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduced ethylene sensitivity markedly increases infection threads and nodule primordia.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A melon ethylene receptor mutated at the ethylene-binding site reduced ethylene sensitivity in transgenic L. japonicus. 2. Transgenic plants produced more infection threads and nodule primordia upon M. loti inoculation. 3. NIN transcript abundance increased in the inoculated transgenic plants, supporting inhibition of nodulation by endogenous ethylene.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This is a review example of heterologous manipulation of ethylene perception rather than a native nodulation gene discovery in Lotus.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating ethylene perception offers a strategy to enhance nodulation in legumes, but broader developmental effects of ethylene insensitivity should be considered.
